perturbation.original_text: The receiving party will protect Confidential Information by using at least the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, to prevent the unauthorized disclosure of such Confidential Information as the receiving party uses to protect its own confidential or proprietary information.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party will endeavor to protect Confidential Information, aiming to use a degree of care it deems adequate, to prevent unauthorized disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'at least the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care' to 'a degree of care it deems adequate' introduces a subjective standard, potentially falling below reasonable care standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota law defines 'Misappropriation' of trade secrets as disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent by a person who used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret, or who at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the knowledge of the trade secret was (i) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. By changing the text to allow a company to determine what care is adequate, it makes trade secrets easier to misappropriate.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.01 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.01 325C.02 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.01 Version List 325C.01 DEFINITIONS. § Subdivision 1. Scope. As used in sections 325C.01 to 325C.07 , the terms defined in these sections have the meanings given them, unless the context requires otherwise. § Subd. 2. Improper means. "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. § Subd. 3. Misappropriation. "Misappropriation" means: (i) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the discloser's or user's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of the discloser's or user's position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. § Subd. 4. Person.
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by allowing the receiving party to use a degree of care it deems 'adequate', contradicts the original text's requirement of 'at least the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care.' This creates a potential for misappropriation, as the 'adequate' care could fall below the standard of reasonable care. According to MN Statute 325C.01 Subd. 3, 'Misappropriation' includes disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent by a person who used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret, or who at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the knowledge of the trade secret was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. By lowering the standard of care, the changed text increases the risk of a breach of the duty to maintain secrecy, potentially leading to misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: Any copies which are made will be identified as belonging to the disclosing party and shall be reproduced with the disclosing party's proprietary rights notices in the same manner in which such notices appear in the original copy provided by the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: Any copies that are created should generally indicate that the original belongs to the disclosing party, and should incorporate any proprietary rights notices, to the extent practical.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'will be identified' and 'shall be reproduced' with 'should generally indicate' and 'should incorporate, to the extent practical' makes the requirement discretionary. The phrase 'to the extent practical' introduces more ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: Copyright law defines the rights of copyright holders to control reproductions of their work. Weakening the requirement to reproduce proprietary rights notices introduces ambiguity and allows for potential copyright infringement by failing to adequately protect the copyright holder's rights. Minnesota adopts the US Code for this.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 U.S. Code § 101 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 101 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next Except as otherwise provided in this title, as used in this title, the following terms and their variant forms mean the following: An “ anonymous work ” is a work on the copies or phonorecords of which no natural person is identified as author. An “ architectural work ” is the design of a building as embodied in any tangible medium of expression, including a building, architectural plans, or drawings. The work includes the overall form as well as the arrangement and composition of spaces and elements in the design, but does not include individual standard features. “ Audiovisual works ” are works that consist of a series of related images which are intrinsically intended to be shown by the use of machines, or devices such as projectors, viewers, or electronic equipment, together with accompanying sounds, if any, regardless of the nature of the material objects, such as films or tapes, in which the works are embodied. The “ Berne Convention ” is the Convention for the Protection of Literary and Artistic Works, signed at Berne, Switzerland, on September 9, 1886 , and all acts, protocols, and revisions thereto. The “ best edition ” of a work is the edition, published in the United States at any time before the date of deposit, that the Library of Congress determines to be most suitable for its purposes. A person’s “ children ” are that person’s immediate offspring, whether legitimate or not, and any children legally adopted by that person. A “ collective work ” is a work, such as a periodical issue, anthology, or encyclopedia, in which a number of contributions, constituting separate and independent works in themselves, are assembled into a collective whole. A “ compilation ” is a work formed by the collection and assembling of pr
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates that copies 'will be identified' and 'shall be reproduced' with proprietary notices. The changed text weakens this obligation by stating copies 'should generally indicate' and 'should incorporate, to the extent practical'. This introduces ambiguity and discretion, making the protection of proprietary rights less stringent. The 'Ambiguity Legal Contradiction' definition states that a legal contradiction arises when an obligation is introduced ambiguously, which aligns with the modification weakening the original mandatory terms. While the snippets don't explicitly define the specifics of reproduction, the law explanation clarifies that this weakening could lead to copyright infringement by not adequately protecting the copyright holder's rights, supporting the existence of a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party will return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confidential Information immediately upon the disclosing party's request or, at the disclosing party's option, destroy the same, and will deliver to the disclosing party, upon its request, a certificate of a duly authorized officer certifying as to the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party should endeavor to return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confidential Information within a reasonable timeframe after the disclosing party's request or, possibly, destroy the same. A good-faith effort should be made to provide certification of these actions.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will return...immediately' to 'should endeavor to return...within a reasonable timeframe' weakens the obligation. Replacing 'will deliver' with 'A good-faith effort should be made' makes certification non-mandatory and subjective.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts the duty to protect trade secrets. By introducing subjective language such as 'reasonable timeframe' and 'good-faith effort', the obligation to return or destroy confidential information becomes vague and unenforceable. This increases the risk of trade secret misappropriation because the disclosing party has less recourse if the receiving party delays or fails to return or destroy the confidential information in a timely and verifiable manner.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.02 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.02 325C.01 325C.03 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.02 Version List 325C.02 INJUNCTIVE RELIEF. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1980 c 594 s 4 ; 1987 c 1 s 1 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the return or destruction of confidential information, weakening the original obligation. The original text states the receiving party 'will return...immediately', while the changed text uses 'should endeavor to return...within a reasonable timeframe', which lacks the force of a strict obligation. Similarly, 'will deliver' is replaced with 'A good-faith effort should be made', making the certification non-mandatory. According to MN Statute 325C.02 (a), 'Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined,' implying a legal right to protect trade secrets. The ambiguous language in the changed text makes it more difficult to enforce this right because the receiving party's obligation is weakened, potentially increasing the risk of misappropriation, which supports the 'Ambiguities Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Minnesota.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be generally interpreted with reference to principles of law that are typically applied in the State of Minnesota.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Minnesota' with 'generally interpreted with reference to principles of law that are typically applied in the State of Minnesota' introduces ambiguity by suggesting a non-binding application of Minnesota law. The lack of clarity on what constitutes 'principles of law that are typically applied' opens the door for subjective interpretations that may conflict with actual Minnesota law.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts Minnesota Statute § 336.1-301(a) which allows parties to agree that the law of a particular state will govern their rights and duties under the contract. The change introduces ambiguity, suggesting that the parties did not actually agree that Minnesota law governs and undermines the parties' ability to agree that Minnesota law applies.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 336.1-301 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 336 Section 336.1-301 336.1-209 336.1-302 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 336 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 336.1-301 Version List Recent History 2024 336.1-301 Amended 2024 c 93 art 1 s 3 2004 336.1-301 New 2004 c 162 art 1 s 16 336.1-301 TERRITORIAL APPLICABILITY; PARTIES' POWER TO CHOOSE APPLICABLE LAW. (a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), the Uniform Commercial Code applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) section 336.2-402 ; (2) sections 336.2A-105 and 336.2A-106 ; (3) section 336.4-102 ; (4) section 336.4A-507 ; (5) section 336.5-116 ; (6) section 336.8-110 ; (7) sections 336.9-301 through 336.9-307 ; (8) section 336.12-107 . History: 2004 c 162 art 1 s 16 ; 2024 c 93 art 1 s 3 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the phrase 'governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Minnesota' with 'generally interpreted with reference to principles of law that are typically applied in the State of Minnesota.' This change weakens the binding effect of Minnesota law on the agreement. Minnesota Statute § 336.1-301(a) states that 'the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties.' The original text establishes a clear agreement that Minnesota law governs the contract. The changed text introduces ambiguity by suggesting Minnesota law is merely a reference point, not a governing authority. This undermines the parties' power to choose the applicable law, as granted by the statute, thus creating a contradiction. The original text's direct application of Minnesota law is replaced with a more flexible interpretation, weakening the legal obligation intended by the statute.
